A **brand** is a name, term, design, symbol or any other feature that distinguishes one seller's goods or service from those of other sellers.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Brands are studied in [business](/wiki/Business "Business"), [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing"), and [advertising](/wiki/Advertising "Advertising") and used for recognition and, importantly, to create and store value as brand equity for the object identified, to the benefit of the brand's customers, its owners and [shareholders](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder").[[6]](#cite_note-6) **Brand names** are sometimes distinguished from [generic](/wiki/Generic_brand "Generic brand") or [store brands](/wiki/Store_brand "Store brand").

The practice of branding—in the original literal sense of marking by burning—is thought to have begun with the [ancient Egyptians](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), who are known to have engaged in [livestock branding](/wiki/Livestock_branding "Livestock branding") and branded slaves as early as 2,700 BCE.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Branding was used to differentiate one person's [cattle](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle") from another's by means of a distinctive symbol burned into the animal's skin with a hot [branding iron](/wiki/Branding_iron "Branding iron"). If a person stole any of the cattle, anyone else who saw the symbol could deduce the actual owner. The etymology of the word “brand” originates from the Old Norse word "brandr" from the 10th Century, which means “to burn". The term "brand" has been extended to mean a strategic personality for a product or company, so that "brand" now suggests the values and promises that a consumer may perceive and buy into. Over time, the practice of branding objects extended to a broader range of packaging and goods offered for sale including [oil](/wiki/Oil "Oil"), [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine"), [cosmetics](/wiki/Cosmetics "Cosmetics"), and [fish sauce](/wiki/Fish_sauce "Fish sauce") and, in the 21st century, extends even further into services (such as [legal](/wiki/Legal_service "Legal service"), [financial](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") and [medical](/wiki/Medical_service "Medical service")), [political parties](/wiki/Political_parties "Political parties") and [people](/wiki/Person "Person")'s stage names.

In the modern era, the concept of branding has expanded to include deployment by a manager of the [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing") and communication techniques and tools that help to distinguish a [company](/wiki/Company "Company") or products from competitors, aiming to create a lasting impression in the minds of [customers](/wiki/Customer "Customer"). The key components that form a brand's toolbox include a brand's identity, personality, [product design](/wiki/Product_design "Product design"), brand communication (such as by [logos](/wiki/Logo "Logo") and [trademarks](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark")), [brand awareness](/wiki/Brand_awareness "Brand awareness"), [brand loyalty](/wiki/Brand_loyalty "Brand loyalty"), and various branding ([brand management](/wiki/Brand_management "Brand management")) strategies.[[9]](#cite_note-Ghodeswar-2008-9) Many companies believe that there is often little to differentiate between several types of products in the 21st century, hence branding is among a few remaining forms of [product differentiation](/wiki/Product_differentiation "Product differentiation").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[Brand equity](/wiki/Brand_equity "Brand equity") is the measurable totality of a brand's worth and is validated by observing the effectiveness of these branding components.[[11]](#cite_note-Keller-1993-11) When a customer is familiar with a brand or favors it incomparably over its competitors, a corporation has reached a high level of brand equity.[[11]](#cite_note-Keller-1993-11) Brand owners manage their brands carefully to create [shareholder value](/wiki/Shareholder_value "Shareholder value"). [Brand valuation](/wiki/Brand_valuation "Brand valuation") is a management technique that ascribes a [monetary value](/wiki/Monetary_value "Monetary value") to a brand.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *brand*, originally meaning a burning piece of wood, comes from a [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") *brand*, meaning "torch",[[12]](#cite_note-Merriam-Webster-1983-12)[[13]](#cite_note-merriam-webster.com-13) from an [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *brand*.[[14]](#cite_note-OED1913_brand-14) It came to also mean the mark from burning with a branding iron.[[12]](#cite_note-Merriam-Webster-1983-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Amphorae_for_wine_and_oil%2C_Archaeological_Museum%2C_Dion_%286934667366%29.jpg/250px-Amphorae_for_wine_and_oil%2C_Archaeological_Museum%2C_Dion_%286934667366%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amphorae_for_wine_and_oil,_Archaeological_Museum,_Dion_(6934667366).jpg)

In pre-literate society, the distinctive shape of [amphorae](/wiki/Amphorae "Amphorae") provided potential customers with information about goods and quality. Pictured: Amphorae for wine and oil, [Archaeological Museum of Dion](/wiki/Archaeological_Museum_of_Dion "Archaeological Museum of Dion").

Branding and labeling have an ancient history. Branding probably began with the practice of [branding livestock](/wiki/Livestock_branding "Livestock branding") to deter theft. Images of the branding of cattle occur in [ancient Egyptian tombs](/wiki/Egyptian_tombs "Egyptian tombs") dating to around 2,700 BCE.[[15]](#cite_note-KhanMufti2007-15) Over time, purchasers realized that the brand provided information about origin as well as about ownership, and could serve as a guide to quality. Branding was adapted by farmers, potters, and traders for use on other types of goods such as pottery and ceramics. Forms of branding or proto-branding emerged spontaneously and independently throughout Africa, Asia and Europe at different times, depending on local conditions.[[16]](#cite_note-civilizations-16) [Seals](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)"), which acted as quasi-brands, have been found on early Chinese products of the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BCE); large numbers of seals survive from the [Harappan civilization](/wiki/Harappan_civilization "Harappan civilization") of the Indus Valley (3,300–1,300 BCE) where the local community depended heavily on trade; [cylinder seals](/wiki/Cylinder_seal "Cylinder seal") came into use in [Ur](/wiki/Ur "Ur") in Mesopotamia in around 3,000 BCE, and facilitated the labelling of goods and property; and the use of maker's marks on pottery was commonplace in both ancient Greece and Rome.[[16]](#cite_note-civilizations-16) Identity marks, such as stamps on ceramics, were also used in ancient Egypt.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Diana Twede has argued that the "consumer packaging functions of protection, utility and communication have been necessary whenever packages were the object of transactions".[[18]](#cite_note-18) She has shown that [amphorae](/wiki/Amphora "Amphora") used in Mediterranean trade between 1,500 and 500 BCE exhibited a wide variety of shapes and markings, which consumers used to glean information about the type of goods and the quality. The systematic use of stamped labels dates from around the fourth century BCE. In largely pre-literate society, the shape of the amphora and its pictorial markings conveyed information about the contents, region of origin and even the identity of the producer, which were understood to convey information about product quality.[[19]](#cite_note-19) [David Wengrow](/wiki/David_Wengrow "David Wengrow") has argued that branding became necessary following the [urban revolution](/wiki/Urban_revolution "Urban revolution") in [ancient Mesopotamia](/wiki/Ancient_Mesopotamia "Ancient Mesopotamia") in the 4th century BCE, when large-scale economies started mass-producing commodities such as alcoholic drinks, cosmetics and textiles. These ancient societies imposed strict forms of quality-control over commodities, and also needed to convey value to the consumer through branding. Producers began by attaching simple stone seals to products which, over time, gave way to clay seals bearing impressed images, often associated with the producer's personal identity thus giving the product a personality.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Not all historians agree that these markings are comparable with modern brands or labels, with some suggesting that the early pictorial brands or simple thumbprints used in pottery should be termed *proto-brands*[[21]](#cite_note-Moore-2008-21) while other historians argue that the presence of these simple markings does not imply that mature [brand management](/wiki/Brand_management "Brand management") practices operated.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Tituli_picti_dressel.jpg/250px-Tituli_picti_dressel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tituli_picti_dressel.jpg)

Amphorae bearing a titulus pictus and potters' stamps, found at Monte Testaccio

Scholarly studies have found evidence of branding, [packaging, and labeling](/wiki/Packaging_and_labeling "Packaging and labeling") in antiquity.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Archaeological evidence of potters' stamps has been found across the breadth of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and in [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"). Stamps were used on bricks, pottery, and storage containers as well as on fine ceramics.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Pottery marking had become commonplace in ancient Greece by the 6th century BCE. A vase manufactured around 490 BCE bears the inscription "[Sophilos](/wiki/Sophilos "Sophilos") painted me", indicating that the object was both fabricated and painted by a single potter.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Branding may have been necessary to support the extensive trade in such pots. For example, 3rd-century Gaulish pots bearing the names of well-known potters and the place of manufacture (such as Attianus of [Lezoux](/wiki/Lezoux "Lezoux"), Tetturo of Lezoux and Cinnamus of [Vichy](/wiki/Vichy "Vichy")) have been found as far away as [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex") and [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall") in England.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) English potters based at Colchester and Chichester used stamps on their ceramic wares by the 1st century CE.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The use of [hallmarks](/wiki/Hallmark "Hallmark"), a type of brand, on precious metals dates to around the 4th century CE. A series of five marks occurs on [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") silver dating from this period.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Trademark_of_Jinan_Liu%27s_Kung-Fu_Needles_Shop.jpg/250px-Trademark_of_Jinan_Liu%27s_Kung-Fu_Needles_Shop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trademark_of_Jinan_Liu%27s_Kung-Fu_Needles_Shop.jpg)

Copper printing-plate including the *White Rabbit* trademark of Jinan Liu's Fine Needles Shop, Chinese, Song Dynasty (960–1127 CE)

Some of the earliest use of maker's marks, dating to about 1,300 BCE, have been found in India.[[15]](#cite_note-KhanMufti2007-15) The oldest generic brand in continuous use, known in India since the [Vedic period](/wiki/Vedic_period "Vedic period") (c. 1100 BCE to 500 BCE), is the herbal paste known as [chyawanprash](/wiki/Chyawanprash "Chyawanprash"), consumed for its purported health benefits and attributed to a revered *[rishi](/wiki/Rishi "Rishi")* (or seer) named Chyawan.[[33]](#cite_note-33) One well-documented early example of a highly developed brand is that of *White Rabbit* sewing needles, dating from China's Song dynasty (960 to 1127 CE).[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) A copper [printing plate](/wiki/Printing "Printing") used to print posters contained a message which roughly translates as: "Jinan Liu's Fine Needle Shop: We buy high-quality steel rods and make fine-quality needles, to be ready for use at home in no time."[[36]](#cite_note-36) The plate also includes a trademark in the form of a "White Rabbit", which signified good luck and was particularly relevant to women, who were the primary purchasers. Details in the image show a white rabbit crushing herbs, and text includes advice to shoppers to look for the stone white rabbit in front of the maker's shop.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Museo_Bellini_-_03-00018300-378_-_Lucerna_romana_-_Black_and_white.jpg/250px-Museo_Bellini_-_03-00018300-378_-_Lucerna_romana_-_Black_and_white.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museo_Bellini_-_03-00018300-378_-_Lucerna_romana_-_Black_and_white.jpg)

Roman oil lamp, showing underside with maker's mark. Museo Bellini

In [ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), a commercial brand or inscription applied to objects offered for sale was known as a [titulus pictus](/wiki/Titulus_pictus "Titulus pictus"). The inscription typically specified information such as place of origin, destination, type of product and occasionally quality claims or the name of the manufacturer.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Roman marks or inscriptions were applied to a very wide variety of goods, including, pots, ceramics, amphorae (storage/shipping containers)[[21]](#cite_note-Moore-2008-21) and on factory-produced oil-lamps.[[39]](#cite_note-39) [Carbonized loaves of bread](/wiki/Herculaneum_loaf "Herculaneum loaf"), found at [Herculaneum](/wiki/Herculaneum "Herculaneum"), indicate that some bakers stamped their bread with the producer's name.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Roman glassmakers branded their works, with the name of [Ennion](/wiki/Ennion "Ennion") appearing most prominently.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Garum_Mosaik_Pompeji.JPG/250px-Garum_Mosaik_Pompeji.JPG)](/wiki/File:Garum_Mosaik_Pompeji.JPG)

Mosaic showing garum container, from the house of [Umbricius Scaurus of Pompeii](/wiki/Aulus_Umbricius_Scaurus "Aulus Umbricius Scaurus"). The inscription, which reads "G(ari) F(los) SCO(mbri) SCAURI EX OFFI(CI)NA SCAURI", has been translated as: "The flower of garum, made of the mackerel, a product of Scaurus, from the shop of Scaurus"

One merchant that made good use of the titulus pictus was Umbricius Scaurus, a manufacturer of fish sauce (also known as [garum](/wiki/Garum "Garum")) in Pompeii, c. 35 CE. Mosaic patterns in the atrium of his house feature images of amphorae bearing his personal brand and quality claims. The mosaic depicts four different amphora, one at each corner of the atrium, and bearing labels as follows:[[42]](#cite_note-Curtis-1984-42)

:   1. G(ari) F(los) SCO[m]/ SCAURI/ EX OFFI[ci]/NA SCAU/RI (translated as: "The flower of garum, made of the mackerel, a product of Scaurus, from the shop of Scaurus")
:   2. LIQU[minis]/ FLOS (translated as: "The flower of Liquamen")
:   3. G[ari] F[los] SCOM[bri]/ SCAURI (translated as: "The flower of garum, made of the mackerel, a product of Scaurus")
:   4. LIQUAMEN/ OPTIMUM/ EX OFFICI[n]/A SCAURI (translated as: "The best liquamen, from the shop of Scaurus")

Scaurus' fish sauce was known by people across the Mediterranean to be of very high quality, and its reputation traveled as far away as modern France.[[42]](#cite_note-Curtis-1984-42) In both Pompeii and nearby Herculaneum, archaeological evidence also points to evidence of branding and labeling in relatively common use across a broad range of goods. Wine jars, for example, were stamped with names, such as "Lassius" and "L. Eumachius"; probably references to the name of the producer.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Hunnish_-_Bracelet_-_Walters_571082_-_Detail_Back.jpg/250px-Hunnish_-_Bracelet_-_Walters_571082_-_Detail_Back.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hunnish_-_Bracelet_-_Walters_571082_-_Detail_Back.jpg)

Back section of a bracelet clasp with a hallmark of Hunnish craftsmanship, early 5th century

The use of identity marks on products declined following the fall of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). In the European [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), [heraldry](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry") developed a language of visual symbolism which would feed into the evolution of branding,[[43]](#cite_note-43)
and with the rise of the [merchant guilds](/wiki/Merchant_guild "Merchant guild") the use of marks resurfaced and was applied to specific types of goods. By the 13th century, the use of maker's marks had become evident on a broad range of goods. In 1266, makers' marks on bread became compulsory in England.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The Italians used brands in the form of [watermarks](/wiki/Watermark "Watermark") on paper in the 13th century.[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Blind stamps](/wiki/Blind_stamp "Blind stamp"), [hallmarks](/wiki/Hallmark "Hallmark"), and [silver-makers' marks](/wiki/Silver_hallmarks "Silver hallmarks")—all types of brand—became widely used across Europe during this period. Hallmarks, although known from the 4th-century, especially in Byzantium,[[46]](#cite_note-46) only came into general use during the Medieval period.[[47]](#cite_note-47) British silversmiths introduced hallmarks for silver in 1300.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Bass_logo.svg/250px-Bass_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bass_logo.svg)

[Bass Brewery](/wiki/Bass_Brewery "Bass Brewery")'s logo became the first image to be registered as a trademark in the UK, in 1876.

Some brands still in existence as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit) date from the 17th, 18th, and 19th centuries' period of mass-production. [Bass Brewery](/wiki/Bass_Brewery "Bass Brewery"), the British brewery founded in 1777, became a pioneer in international brand marketing. Many years before 1855, Bass applied a red triangle to casks of its pale ale. In 1876, its red-triangle brand became the first registered [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") issued by the British government.[[49]](#cite_note-49) *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* recognizes [Tate & Lyle](/wiki/Tate_%26_Lyle "Tate & Lyle") (of [Lyle's Golden Syrup](/wiki/Lyle%27s_Golden_Syrup "Lyle's Golden Syrup")) as Britain's, and the world's, oldest branding and packaging, with its green-and-gold packaging having remained almost unchanged since 1885.[[50]](#cite_note-50) [Twinings tea](/wiki/Twinings_tea "Twinings tea") has used the same logo – capitalized font beneath a lion crest – since 1787, making it the world's oldest in continuous use.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Lyle%27sGoldenSyrup.jpg/250px-Lyle%27sGoldenSyrup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyle%27sGoldenSyrup.jpg)

A tin of Lyle's [Golden Syrup](/wiki/Golden_Syrup "Golden Syrup"), first sold in London in 1885. Recognised by *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* as having the world's oldest branding and packaging.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

A characteristic feature of 19th-century mass-marketing was the widespread use of branding, originating with the advent of packaged [goods](/wiki/Good_(economics) "Good (economics)").[[15]](#cite_note-KhanMufti2007-15) [Industrialization](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") moved the production of many household items, such as [soap](/wiki/Soap "Soap"), from local communities to centralized [factories](/wiki/Factory "Factory"). When shipping their items, the factories would literally [brand](/wiki/Wood_branding "Wood branding") their [logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo") or company insignia on the barrels used, effectively using a corporate trademark as a quasi-brand.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Factories established following the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") introduced mass-produced goods and needed to sell their products to a wider market—that is, to customers previously familiar only with locally produced goods.[[55]](#cite_note-55) It became apparent that a generic package of soap had difficulty competing with familiar, local products. Packaged-goods manufacturers needed to convince the market that the public could place just as much trust in the non-local product. Gradually, manufacturers began using personal identifiers to differentiate their goods from generic products on the market. Marketers generally began to realize that brands, to which personalities were attached, outsold rival brands.[[56]](#cite_note-56) By the 1880s, large manufacturers had learned to imbue their brands' [identity](/wiki/Brand_identity "Brand identity") with [personality](/wiki/Personality "Personality") traits such as youthfulness, fun, sex appeal, luxury or the "cool" factor. This began the modern practice now known as *branding*, where the consumers buy *the brand* instead of the product and rely on the brand name instead of a retailer's recommendation.

The process of giving a brand "human" characteristics represented, at least in part, a response to consumer concerns about mass-produced goods.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The [Quaker Oats Company](/wiki/Quaker_Oats_Company "Quaker Oats Company") began using the image of the Quaker Man in place of a trademark from the late 1870s, with great success.[[58]](#cite_note-58) [Pears' soap](/wiki/Pears_(soap) "Pears (soap)"), [Campbell's soup](/wiki/Campbell_Soup_Company "Campbell Soup Company"), [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola"), [Juicy Fruit](/wiki/Juicy_Fruit "Juicy Fruit") chewing gum and [Aunt Jemima](/wiki/Aunt_Jemima "Aunt Jemima") pancake mix were also among the first products to be "branded" in an effort to increase the consumer's familiarity with the product's merits. Other brands which date from that era, such as [Ben's Original](/wiki/Uncle_Ben%27s "Uncle Ben's") rice and [Kellogg's](/wiki/Kellogg%27s "Kellogg's") breakfast cereal, furnish illustrations of the trend.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Quaker_Oats_%283092914571%29.jpg/250px-Quaker_Oats_%283092914571%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quaker_Oats_(3092914571).jpg)

The Quaker Company was one of the earliest to use a character on its packaging, branding, and advertising. Pictured: The Quaker Man, c. 1900

By the early 1900s, [trade press](/wiki/Trade_press "Trade press") publications, [advertising agencies](/wiki/Advertising_agency "Advertising agency"), and advertising experts began producing books and pamphlets exhorting manufacturers to bypass retailers and to advertise directly to consumers with strongly branded messages. Around 1900, advertising guru [James Walter Thompson](/wiki/James_Walter_Thompson "James Walter Thompson") published a housing advertisement explaining trademark advertising. This was an early commercial explanation of what scholars now recognize as modern branding and the beginnings of brand management.[[59]](#cite_note-59) This trend continued to the 1980s, and as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit) is quantified by marketers in concepts such as [brand value](/wiki/Value_(ethics) "Value (ethics)") and [brand equity](/wiki/Brand_equity "Brand equity").[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Naomi Klein](/wiki/Naomi_Klein "Naomi Klein") has described this development as "brand equity mania".[[61]](#cite_note-nologo-61) In 1988, for example, [Philip Morris Companies](/wiki/Philip_Morris_Companies "Philip Morris Companies") purchased [Kraft Foods Inc.](/wiki/Kraft_Foods_Inc. "Kraft Foods Inc.") for six times what the company was worth on paper. Business analysts reported that what they really purchased was the brand name.

With the rise of [mass media](/wiki/Mass_media "Mass media") in the early 20th century, [companies](/wiki/Company "Company") adopted techniques that allowed their messages to stand out. [Slogans](/wiki/Slogan "Slogan"), [mascots](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot"), and [jingles](/wiki/Jingle "Jingle") began to appear on [radio in the 1920s](/w/index.php?title=1920s_in_radio&action=edit&redlink=1 "1920s in radio (page does not exist)") and in early [television in the 1930s](/wiki/1930s_in_the_United_States "1930s in the United States"). Soap manufacturers sponsored many of the earliest [radio drama](/wiki/Radio_drama "Radio drama") series, and the genre became known as *[soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera")*.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

By the 1940s, manufacturers began to recognize the way in which consumers had started to develop relationships with their brands in a social/psychological/anthropological sense.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Advertisers began to use motivational research and [consumer research](/wiki/Consumer_research "Consumer research") to gather insights into consumer purchasing. Strong branded campaigns for [Chrysler](/wiki/Chrysler "Chrysler") and [Exxon](/wiki/Exxon "Exxon")/Esso, using insights drawn from research into psychology and [cultural anthropology](/wiki/Cultural_anthropology "Cultural anthropology"), led to some of the most enduring campaigns of the 20th century.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Brand advertisers began to imbue goods and services with a personality, based on the insight that consumers searched for brands with personalities that matched their own.[[65]](#cite_note-Aaker-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Concepts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Concepts")]

Effective branding, attached to strong brand values, can result in higher sales of not only one product, but of other products associated with that brand.[[67]](#cite_note-67) If a customer loves Pillsbury biscuits and trusts the brand, he or she is more likely to try other products offered by the company – such as chocolate-chip cookies, for example. Brand development, often performed by a [design](/wiki/Design "Design") [team](/wiki/Team "Team"), takes time to produce.

### Brand names and trademarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Brand names and trademarks")]

Further information: [Product naming](/wiki/Product_naming "Product naming"), [Trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark"), and [Trademark symbol](/wiki/Trademark_symbol "Trademark symbol")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/CocaColaBottle.jpg/250px-CocaColaBottle.jpg)](/wiki/File:CocaColaBottle.jpg)

*[Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola")* is a brand name, while the distinctive [Spencerian script](/wiki/Spencerian_script "Spencerian script") and the [contour bottle](/wiki/Contour_bottle "Contour bottle") are [trademarked](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark").

A brand name is the part of a brand that can be spoken or written and *identifies* a product, service or company and sets it apart from other comparable products within a category. A brand name may include words, phrases, signs, symbols, designs, or any combination of these elements. For consumers, a brand name is a "memory heuristic": a convenient way to remember preferred product choices. A brand name is not to be confused with a *trademark* which refers to the brand name or part of a brand that is legally protected.[[68]](#cite_note-68) For example, Coca-Cola not only protects the brand name, *Coca-Cola*, but also protects the distinctive Spencerian script and the contoured shape of the bottle.

### Corporate brand identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Corporate brand identity")]

Brand identity is a collection of individual components, such as a name, a design, a set of images, a slogan, a vision, writing style, a particular font or a symbol etc. which sets the brand aside from others.[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)
For a company to exude a strong sense of brand identity, it must have an in-depth understanding of its target market, competitors and the surrounding business environment.[[9]](#cite_note-Ghodeswar-2008-9) Brand identity includes both the core identity and the extended identity.[[9]](#cite_note-Ghodeswar-2008-9) The core identity reflects consistent long-term associations with the brand; whereas the extended identity involves the intricate details of the brand that help generate a constant motif.[[9]](#cite_note-Ghodeswar-2008-9)

According to Kotler et al. (2009), a brand's identity may deliver 4 levels of meaning:

1. attributes
2. benefits
3. values
4. personality

A brand's **attributes** are a set of labels with which the corporation wishes to be associated. For example, a brand may showcase its primary attribute as environmental friendliness. However, a brand's attributes alone are not enough to persuade a customer into purchasing the product.[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69) These attributes must be communicated through **benefits**, which are more emotional translations. If a brand's attribute is being environmentally friendly, customers will receive the benefit of feeling that they are helping the environment by associating with the brand. Aside from attributes and benefits, a brand's identity may also involve branding to focus on representing its core set of **values**.[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69) If a company is seen to symbolize specific values, it will, in turn, attract customers who also believe in these values.[[65]](#cite_note-Aaker-65) For example, Nike's brand represents the value of a "[just do it](/wiki/Just_Do_It "Just Do It")" attitude.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Thus, this form of brand identification attracts customers who also share this same value. Even more extensive than its perceived values is a brand's **personality**.[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69) Quite literally, one can easily describe a successful brand identity as if it were a person.[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69) This form of brand identity has proven to be the most advantageous in maintaining long-lasting relationships with consumers, as it gives them a sense of personal interaction with the brand [[72]](#cite_note-Madhavaram-2005-72) Collectively, all four forms of brand identification help to deliver a powerful meaning behind what a corporation hopes to accomplish, and to explain why customers should choose one brand over its competitors.[[9]](#cite_note-Ghodeswar-2008-9)

### Brand personality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Brand personality")]

**Brand personality** refers to "the set of human personality traits that are both applicable to and relevant for brands."[[73]](#cite_note-73) Marketers and consumer researchers often argue that brands can be imbued with human-like characteristics which resonate with potential consumers.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Such personality traits can assist marketers to create unique, brands that are differentiated from rival brands. Aaker conceptualized brand personality as consisting of five broad dimensions, namely: sincerity (down-to-earth, honest, wholesome, and cheerful), excitement (daring, spirited, imaginative, and up to date), competence (reliable, intelligent, and successful), sophistication (glamorous, upper class, charming), and ruggedness (outdoorsy and tough).[[75]](#cite_note-75) Subsequent research studies have suggested that Aaker's dimensions of brand personality are relatively stable across different industries, market segments and over time. Much of the literature on branding suggests that consumers prefer brands with personalities that are congruent with their own.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Consumers may distinguish the psychological aspect (brand associations like thoughts, feelings, perceptions, images, experiences, beliefs, attitudes, and so on that become linked to the brand) of a brand from the experiential aspect. The experiential aspect consists of the sum of all points of contact with the brand and is termed the consumer's *[brand experience](/wiki/Brand_experience "Brand experience")*. The brand is often intended to create an emotional response and recognition, leading to potential loyalty and repeat purchases. The brand experience is a brand's action perceived by a person.[[78]](#cite_note-Brakus-78) The psychological aspect, sometimes referred to as the *brand image*, is a symbolic construct created within the minds of people, consisting of all the information and expectations associated with a product, with a service, or with the companies providing them.[[78]](#cite_note-Brakus-78)

Marketers or product managers responsible for branding seek to develop or align the expectations behind the brand experience. Their goal is to create the impression that a brand associated with a product or service has certain qualities or characteristics which make it special or unique.[[79]](#cite_note-Kotler-2012-79) Therefore, a brand can become one of the most valuable elements in an advertising strategy as it demonstrates what the brand owner is able to offer in the [marketplace](/wiki/Market_(place) "Market (place)"). This means that building a strong brand helps to distinguish a product from similar ones and sets it apart from competitors.[[65]](#cite_note-Aaker-65) The art of creating and maintaining a brand is called [brand management](/wiki/Brand_management "Brand management"). The orientation of an entire organization towards its brand is called [brand orientation](/wiki/Brand_orientation "Brand orientation"). Brand orientation develops in response to [market intelligence](/wiki/Market_intelligence "Market intelligence").[[79]](#cite_note-Kotler-2012-79)

Careful brand management seeks to make products or services relevant and meaningful to a [target audience](/wiki/Target_audience "Target audience"). Marketers tend to treat brands as more than the difference between the actual cost of a product and its selling price; rather brands represent the sum of all valuable qualities of a product to the consumer and are often treated as the total investment in brand building activities including marketing communications.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Consumers may look on branding as an aspect of products or services,[[11]](#cite_note-Keller-1993-11) as it often serves to denote a certain attractive quality or characteristic (see also brand promise). From the perspective of brand owners, branded products or services can command higher prices. Where two products resemble each other, but one of the products has no associated branding (such as a [generic](/wiki/Generic_brand "Generic brand"), store-branded product), potential purchasers may often select the more expensive branded product on the basis of the perceived quality of the brand or on the basis of the reputation of the brand owner.

### Brand awareness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Brand awareness")]

Main article: [Brand awareness](/wiki/Brand_awareness "Brand awareness")

[Brand awareness](/wiki/Brand_awareness "Brand awareness") involves a customer's ability to recall and/or recognize brands, logos, and branded advertising. Brands help customers to understand which brands or products belong to which product or service category. Brands assist customers to understand the constellation of benefits offered by individual brands, and how a given brand within a category is differentiated from its competing brands, and thus the brand helps customers & potential customers understand which brand satisfies their needs. Thus, the brand offers the customer a short-cut to understanding the different product or service offerings that make up a particular category.

Brand awareness is a key step in the customer's purchase decision process, since some kind of awareness is a precondition to purchasing. That is, customers will not consider a brand if they are not aware of it.[[81]](#cite_note-81) Brand awareness is a key component in understanding the effectiveness both of a brand's identity and of its communication methods.[[82]](#cite_note-Chitty-2005-82) Successful brands are those that consistently generate a high level of brand awareness, as this can be the pivotal factor in securing customer transactions.[[83]](#cite_note-Percy-1992-83) Various forms of brand awareness can be identified. Each form reflects a different stage in a customer's cognitive ability to address the brand in a given circumstance.[[11]](#cite_note-Keller-1993-11)

Marketers typically identify two distinct types of brand awareness; namely *brand recall* (also known as *unaided recall* or occasionally *spontaneous recall*) and *brand recognition* (also known as *aided brand recall*).[[84]](#cite_note-84) These types of awareness operate in entirely different ways with important implications for marketing strategy and advertising.

* Most companies aim for "**Top-of-Mind**" which occurs when a brand pops into a consumer's mind when asked to name brands in a product category. For example, when someone is asked to name a type of facial tissue, the common answer, "Kleenex", will represent a top-of-mind brand. Top-of-mind awareness is a special case of brand recall.
* **Brand recall** (also known as *unaided brand awareness* or *spontaneous awareness*) refers to the brand or set of brands that a consumer can elicit from memory when prompted with a product category
* **Brand recognition** (also known as *aided brand awareness*) occurs when consumers see or read a list of brands, and express familiarity with a particular brand only after they hear or see it as a type of memory aide.
* **Strategic awareness** occurs when a brand is not only top-of-mind to consumers, but also has distinctive qualities which consumers perceive as making it better than other brands in the particular market. The distinction(s) that set a product apart from the competition is/are also known as the [unique selling point](/wiki/Unique_selling_proposition "Unique selling proposition") or USP.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Brand recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Brand recognition")]

**Brand recognition** is one of the initial phases of brand awareness and validates whether or not a customer remembers being pre-exposed to the brand.[[83]](#cite_note-Percy-1992-83) Brand recognition (also known as *aided brand recall*) refers to consumers' ability to correctly differentiate a brand when they come into contact with it. This does not necessarily require consumers to identify or recall the brand name. When customers experience brand recognition, they are triggered by either a visual or verbal cue.[[11]](#cite_note-Keller-1993-11) For example, when looking to satisfy a category need such as a toilet paper, the customer would firstly be presented with multiple brands to choose from. Once the customer is visually or verbally faced with a brand, they may remember being introduced to it before. When given a cue, consumers able to retrieve the memory node associated with the brand exhibit brand recognition.[[11]](#cite_note-Keller-1993-11) Often, this form of brand awareness assists customers in choosing one brand over another when faced with a low-involvement purchasing decision.[[86]](#cite_note-MacDonald-2000-86)

Brand recognition is often the mode of brand awareness that operates in [retail](/wiki/Retail "Retail") shopping environments. When presented with a product at the point-of-sale, or after viewing its visual packaging, consumers are able to recognize the brand and may be able to associate it with attributes or meanings acquired through exposure to promotion or word-of-mouth referrals.[[87]](#cite_note-87) In contrast to brand recall, where few consumers are able to spontaneously recall brand names within a given category, when prompted with a brand name, a larger number of consumers are typically able to recognize it.

Brand recognition is most successful when people can elicit recognition without being explicitly exposed to the company's name, but rather through visual signifiers like logos, slogans, and colors.[[88]](#cite_note-88) For example, [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") successfully branded its particular script font (originally created for Walt Disney's "signature" [logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo")), which it used in the logo for [go.com](/wiki/Go.com "Go.com").

### Brand recall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Brand recall")]

Unlike brand recognition, **brand recall** (also known as *unaided brand recall* or *spontaneous brand recall*) is the ability of the customer retrieving the brand correctly from memory.[[11]](#cite_note-Keller-1993-11) Rather than being given a choice of multiple brands to satisfy a need, consumers are faced with a need first, and then must recall a brand from their memory to satisfy that need. This level of brand awareness is stronger than brand recognition, as the brand must be firmly cemented in the consumer's memory to enable unassisted remembrance.[[83]](#cite_note-Percy-1992-83) This gives the company huge advantage over its competitors because the customer is already willing to buy or at least know the company offering available in the market. Thus, brand recall is a confirmation that previous branding [touchpoints](/wiki/Touchpoint "Touchpoint") have successfully fermented in the minds of its consumers.[[86]](#cite_note-MacDonald-2000-86)

[Marketing-mix modeling](/wiki/Marketing_mix_modeling "Marketing mix modeling") can help marketing leaders optimize how they spend marketing budgets to maximize the impact on brand awareness or on sales. Managing brands for [value creation](/wiki/Value_proposition "Value proposition") will often involve applying marketing-mix modeling techniques in conjunction with [brand valuation](/wiki/Brand_valuation "Brand valuation").[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69)

## Brand elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Brand elements")]

Brands typically comprise various elements, such as:[[89]](#cite_note-89)

* name: the word or words used to identify a company, product, service, or concept
* logo: the visual trademark that identifies a brand
* tagline or catchphrase: a short phrase always used in the product's advertising and closely associated with the brand
* graphics: the "dynamic ribbon" is a trademarked part of Coca-Cola's brand
* shapes: the distinctive shapes of the Coca-Cola bottle and of the Volkswagen Beetle are trademarked elements of those brands
* colors: the instant recognition consumers have when they see [Tiffany & Co.'s robin's egg blue](/wiki/Tiffany_Blue "Tiffany Blue") (Pantone No. 1837). Tiffany & Co.'s trademarked the color in 1998.[[90]](#cite_note-90)
* sounds: a unique tune or set of notes can denote a brand. [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s chimes provide a famous example.
* scents: the rose-jasmine-musk scent of [Chanel No. 5](/wiki/Chanel_No._5 "Chanel No. 5") is trademarked
* tastes: [Kentucky Fried Chicken](/wiki/KFC "KFC") has trademarked its special recipe of eleven herbs and spices for fried chicken
* movements: [Lamborghini](/wiki/Lamborghini "Lamborghini") has trademarked the upward motion of its car doors
* voice: the expression of a company's personality through its words across channels such as their site copy, advertising, customer emails, newsletters, etc.

### Brand communication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Brand communication")]

Further information: [Advertising management](/wiki/Advertising_management "Advertising management"), [Integrated marketing communications](/wiki/Integrated_marketing_communications "Integrated marketing communications"), [Marketing communications](/wiki/Marketing_communications "Marketing communications"), and [Promotion (marketing)](/wiki/Promotion_(marketing) "Promotion (marketing)")

Although brand identity is a fundamental asset to a brand's [equity](/wiki/Brand_equity "Brand equity"), the worth of a brand's identity would become obsolete without ongoing brand communication.[[91]](#cite_note-91) [Integrated marketing communications](/wiki/Integrated_marketing_communications "Integrated marketing communications") (IMC) relates to how a brand transmits a clear consistent message to its [stakeholders](/wiki/Stakeholder_(corporate) "Stakeholder (corporate)") .[[82]](#cite_note-Chitty-2005-82) Five key components comprise IMC:[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69)

1. Advertising
2. Sales promotions
3. Direct marketing
4. Personal selling
5. Public relations

The effectiveness of a brand's communication is determined by how accurately the customer perceives the brand's intended message through its IMC. Although IMC is a broad strategic concept, the most crucial brand communication elements are pinpointed to how the brand sends a message and what touch points the brand uses to connect with its customers [Chitty 2005].[[82]](#cite_note-Chitty-2005-82)

One can analyze the traditional communication model into several consecutive steps:[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69)

* Firstly, a source/sender wishes to convey a message to a receiver. This source must encode the intended message in a way that the receiver will potentially understand.[[82]](#cite_note-Chitty-2005-82)
* After the encoding stage, the forming of the message is complete and is portrayed through a selected channel.[[92]](#cite_note-Belch-2012-92) In IMC, channels may include media elements such as advertising, public relations, sales promotions, etc.[[82]](#cite_note-Chitty-2005-82)
* It is at this point where the message can often deter from its original purpose as the message must go through the process of being decoded, which can often lead to unintended misinterpretation.[[92]](#cite_note-Belch-2012-92)
* Finally, the receiver retrieves the message and attempts to understand what the sender was aiming to render. Often, a message may be incorrectly received due to noise in the market, which is caused by "…unplanned static or distortion during the communication process".[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69)
* The final stage of this process is when the receiver responds to the message, which is received by the original sender as feedback.[[72]](#cite_note-Madhavaram-2005-72)

When a brand communicates a brand identity to a receiver, it runs the risk of the receiver incorrectly interpreting the message. Therefore, a brand should use appropriate communication channels to positively "…affect how the psychological and physical aspects of a brand are perceived".[[93]](#cite_note-Dahlen-2010-93)

In order for brands to effectively communicate to customers, marketers must "…consider all [touch point](/wiki/Touchpoint "Touchpoint")|s, or sources of contact, that a customer has with the brand".[[94]](#cite_note-Shimp-2009-94) Touch points represent the channel stage in the traditional communication model, where a message travels from the sender to the receiver. Any point where a customer has an interaction with the brand - whether watching a television advertisement, hearing about a brand through word of mouth or even noticing a branded license plate – defines a touchpoint. According to Dahlen *et al.* (2010), every touchpoint has the "…potential to add positive – or suppress negative – associations to the brand's equity"[[93]](#cite_note-Dahlen-2010-93) Thus, a brand's IMC should cohesively deliver positive messages through appropriate touch points associated with its target market. One methodology involves using sensory stimuli touch points to activate customer emotion.[[94]](#cite_note-Shimp-2009-94) For example, if a brand consistently uses a pleasant smell as a primary touchpoint, the brand has a much higher chance of creating a positive lasting effect on its customers' senses as well as memory.[[72]](#cite_note-Madhavaram-2005-72) Another way a brand can ensure that it is utilizing the best communication channel is by focusing on touchpoints that suit particular areas associated with [customer experience](/wiki/Customer_experience "Customer experience").[[69]](#cite_note-Kotler-2009-69) As suggested Figure 2, certain touch points link with a specific stage in customer-brand-involvement. For example, a brand may recognize that advertising touchpoints are most effective during the pre-purchase experience stage therefore they may target their advertisements to new customers rather than to existing customers. Overall, a brand has the ability to strengthen brand equity by using IMC branding communications through touchpoints.[[94]](#cite_note-Shimp-2009-94)

Brand communication is important in ensuring brand success in the [business world](/wiki/Business_sector "Business sector") and refers to how businesses transmit their brand messages, characteristics and attributes to their [consumers](/wiki/Consumer "Consumer").[[95]](#cite_note-95) One method of brand communication that companies can exploit involves electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM). eWOM is a relatively new approach [Phelps et al., 2004][[96]](#cite_note-96) identified to communicate with consumers. One popular method of eWOM involves [social networking sites](/wiki/Social_networking_service "Social networking service") (SNSs) such as [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter").[[97]](#cite_note-97) A study found that consumers classed their relationship with a brand as closer if that brand was active on a specific social media site (Twitter). Research further found that the more consumers "retweeted" and communicated with a brand, the more they trusted the brand. This suggests that a company could look to employ a [social-media campaign](/wiki/Social-media_marketing "Social-media marketing") to gain consumer trust and loyalty as well as in the pursuit of communicating brand messages.

McKee (2014) also looked into brand communication and states that when communicating a brand, a company should look to simplify its message as this will lead to more value being portrayed as well as an increased chance of target consumers recalling and recognizing the brand.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

In 2012 Riefler stated that if the company communicating a brand is a global organization or has future global aims, that company should look to employ a method of communication that is globally appealing to their consumers, and subsequently choose a method of communication with will be internationally understood.[[99]](#cite_note-Riefler,_P._2012._pp.25-99) One way a company can do this involves choosing a product or service's brand name, as this name will need to be suitable for the marketplace that it aims to enter.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

It is important that if a company wishes to develop a global market, the company name will also need to be suitable in different cultures and not cause offense or be misunderstood.[[101]](#cite_note-101) When communicating a brand, a company needs to be aware that they must not just visually communicate their brand message and should take advantage of portraying their message through multi-sensory information.[[102]](#cite_note-102) One article suggests that other senses, apart from vision, need to be targeted when trying to communicate a brand with consumers.[[103]](#cite_note-103) For example, a jingle or background music can have a positive effect on brand recognition, purchasing behaviour and brand recall.

Therefore, when looking to communicate a brand with chosen consumers, companies should investigate a channel of communication that is most suitable for their short-term and long-term aims and should choose a method of communication that is most likely to reach their target consumers.[[99]](#cite_note-Riefler,_P._2012._pp.25-99) The match-up between the product, the consumer lifestyle, and the endorser is important for the effectiveness of brand communication.

## Global brand variables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Global brand variables")]

### Brand name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Brand name")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Relationships_trade_marks_%E2%80%93_brand.svg/250px-Relationships_trade_marks_%E2%80%93_brand.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Relationships_trade_marks_%E2%80%93_brand.svg)

Relationship between trademarks and brand

The term "brand name" is quite often used interchangeably with "brand", although it is more correctly used to specifically denote written or spoken linguistic elements of any product. In this context, a "brand name" constitutes a type of [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark"), if the brand name exclusively identifies the brand owner as the commercial source of products or services. A brand owner may seek to protect [proprietary rights](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") in relation to a brand name through [trademark registration](/wiki/Trademark_registration "Trademark registration") – such trademarks are called "Registered Trademarks". Advertising spokespersons have also become part of some brands, for example: [Mr. Whipple](/wiki/Mr._Whipple "Mr. Whipple") of [Charmin](/wiki/Charmin "Charmin") toilet tissue and [Tony the Tiger](/wiki/Tony_the_Tiger "Tony the Tiger") of [Kellogg's Frosted Flakes](/wiki/Frosted_Flakes "Frosted Flakes"). Putting a value on a brand by [brand valuation](/wiki/Brand_valuation "Brand valuation") or using [marketing mix modeling](/wiki/Marketing_mix_modeling "Marketing mix modeling") techniques is distinct to valuing a [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark").

[US government purchasing](/wiki/Government_procurement_in_the_United_States "Government procurement in the United States") rules allow for [federal departments](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") to seek bids using a "brand name or equal" provision in their [solicitation](/wiki/Invitation_to_tender "Invitation to tender"). [Federal Acquisition Regulation](/wiki/Federal_Acquisition_Regulation "Federal Acquisition Regulation") 52.211-6 states that "where an item in [a] solicitation is identified as "brand name or equal", the purchase description [should] reflect the characteristics and level of quality that will satisfy the Government's needs. The salient physical, functional, or performance characteristics that "equal" products must meet are [to be] specified in the solicitation.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

#### Types of brand names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Types of brand names")]

Brand names come in many styles.[[105]](#cite_note-105) These include:

* **[initialism](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym")**: a name made of initials, such as "[UPS](/wiki/United_Parcel_Service "United Parcel Service")" or "[IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM")"
* **descriptive**: names that describe a product benefit or function, such as "[Whole Foods](/wiki/Whole_Foods_Market "Whole Foods Market")" or "[Toys R' Us](/wiki/Toys_%22R%22_Us "Toys \"R\" Us")"
* **[alliteration](/wiki/Alliteration "Alliteration") and [rhyme](/wiki/Rhyme "Rhyme")**: names that are fun to say and which stick in the mind, such as "[Reese's Pieces](/wiki/Reese%27s_Pieces "Reese's Pieces")" or "[Dunkin' Donuts](/wiki/Dunkin%27_Donuts "Dunkin' Donuts")"
* **evocative**: names that can evoke a vivid image, such as "[Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)")" or "[Crest](/wiki/Crest_(toothpaste) "Crest (toothpaste)")"
* **[neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism")**: completely made-up words, such as "[Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii")" or "[Häagen-Dazs](/wiki/H%C3%A4agen-Dazs "Häagen-Dazs")"
* **foreign word**: adoption of a word from another language, such as "[Volvo](/wiki/Volvo_Cars "Volvo Cars")"
* **founders' names**: using the names of real people, (especially a founder's surname), such as "[Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard")", "[Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell")", "[Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company")", "[Stussy](/wiki/St%C3%BCssy "Stüssy")" or "[Mars](/wiki/Mars,_Incorporated "Mars, Incorporated")"
* **geography**: naming for regions and landmarks, such as "[Cisco](/wiki/Cisco_Systems "Cisco Systems")" or "[Fuji Film](/wiki/Fujifilm "Fujifilm")"
* **[personification](/wiki/Personification "Personification")**: taking names from myths, such as ["Nike"](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc."); or from the minds of ad execs, such as "[Betty Crocker](/wiki/Betty_Crocker "Betty Crocker")"
* **[punny](/wiki/Pun "Pun")**: some brands create their name by using a silly pun, such as "[Lord of the Fries](/wiki/Lord_of_the_Fries "Lord of the Fries")", "Wok on Water" or "Eggs Eggscetera"
* **[portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau")**: combining multiple words together to create one, such as "[Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft")" ("microcomputer" and "software"), "[Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast")" ("communications" and "broadcast"), "[Evernote](/wiki/Evernote "Evernote")" ("forever" and "note"), "[Vodafone](/wiki/Vodafone "Vodafone")" ("voice", "data", "telephone")

The act of associating a product or service with a brand has become part of [pop culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"). Most products have some kind of brand identity, from common [table salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt") to [designer jeans](/wiki/Designer_clothing "Designer clothing"). A [brandnomer](/wiki/Generic_trademark "Generic trademark") is a brand name that has [colloquially](/wiki/Colloquialism "Colloquialism") become a generic term for a product or service, such as [Band-Aid](/wiki/Band-Aid "Band-Aid"), [Nylon](/wiki/Nylon "Nylon"), or [Kleenex](/wiki/Kleenex "Kleenex")—which are often used to describe any brand of adhesive bandage; any type of hosiery; or any brand of facial tissue respectively. [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox"), for example, has become [synonymous](/wiki/Synonym "Synonym") with the word "copy".

### Brand line

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Brand line")]

A brand line allows the introduction of various subtypes of a product under a common, ideally already established, brand name. Examples would be the individual [Kinder](/wiki/Kinder_(brand) "Kinder (brand)") chocolates by [Ferrero SpA](/wiki/Ferrero_SpA "Ferrero SpA"), the subtypes of [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola"), or [special editions](/wiki/Special_edition "Special edition") of popular brands. See also [brand extension](#Brand_extension_and_brand_dilution).

[Open Knowledge Foundation](/wiki/Open_Knowledge_International "Open Knowledge International") created in December 2013 the [BSIN](/wiki/BSIN "BSIN") (Brand Standard Identification Number). [BSIN](/wiki/BSIN "BSIN") is universal and is used by the Open Product Data Working Group [[106]](#cite_note-106) of the [Open Knowledge Foundation](/wiki/Open_Knowledge_International "Open Knowledge International") to assign a brand to a product. The OKFN Brand repository is critical for the Open Data movement.

### Brand identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Brand identity")]

The expression of a brand – including its name, trademark, communications, and visual appearance – is brand identity.[[107]](#cite_note-Dictionary_of_Brand-107) Because the identity is assembled by the brand owner, it reflects how the owner *wants* the consumer to perceive the brand – and by extension the branded company, organization, product or service. This is in contrast to the brand image, which is a customer's mental picture of a brand.[[107]](#cite_note-Dictionary_of_Brand-107) The brand owner will seek to bridge the gap between the brand image and the brand identity. Brand identity is fundamental to consumer recognition and symbolizes the brand's differentiation from competitors. Brand identity is distinct from brand image.

Brand identity is what the owner wants to communicate to its potential consumers. However, over time, a product's brand identity may acquire (evolve), gaining new attributes from consumer perspective but not necessarily from the marketing communications, an owner percolates to targeted consumers. Therefore, businesses research consumer's brand associations.

The brand identity works as a guideline, as the frame in which a brand will evolve and define itself, or in the words of David Aaker, "…a unique set of brand associations that the brand strategist aspires to create or maintain."[[108]](#cite_note-108)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/2013-07-19_grow-your-own-ideas_by-David-Revoy.jpg/330px-2013-07-19_grow-your-own-ideas_by-David-Revoy.jpg)](/wiki/File:2013-07-19_grow-your-own-ideas_by-David-Revoy.jpg)

One of the facets to a brand's identity is self-image: How one brand-customer portrays their ideal self – how they want to look and behave; what they aspire to – brands can target their messaging accordingly and make the brand's aspirations reflect theirs.

According to Kapferer (2004), there are six facets to a brand's identity:[[109]](#cite_note-109)

* Physique: The physical characteristics and iconography of your brand (such as the Nike swoosh or the orange pantone of easyJet).
* Personality: The persona, how a brand communicates with their audience, which is expressed through its tone of voice, design assets and then integrates this into communication touchpoints in a coherent way.
* Culture: The values, the principles on which a brand bases its behaviour. For example, Google flexible office hours and fun environment so the employees feel happy and creative at work.
* Reflection: The "stereotypical user" of the brand. A brand is likely to be purchased by several buyer's profiles but they will have a go-to person that they use in their campaigns. For example, Lou Yetu and the Parisian chic profile.
* Relationship: The bond between a brand and its customers, and the customer expectations of the brand (the experience beyond the tangible product). Such as warranties or services during and after purchase help maintain a sustainable relationship and keep the consumer trust.
* Self-image: How one brand-customer portrays their ideal self – how they want to look and behave; what they aspire to – brands can target their messaging accordingly and make the brand's aspirations reflect theirs.

### Brand image

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Brand image")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Nizhny_Novgorod_Macbook_%28Unsplash%29.jpg/250px-Nizhny_Novgorod_Macbook_%28Unsplash%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nizhny_Novgorod_Macbook_(Unsplash).jpg)

**Apple:** Known for innovation, sleek design, and premium quality, Apple's brand image appeals to consumers seeking cutting-edge technology.

Brand image refers to the perception consumers have about a brand, shaped by their interactions, experiences, and beliefs regarding the brand's products or services. It's the impression that resides in the minds of consumers, influencing their purchasing decisions and loyalty.[[110]](#cite_note-:0-110)

#### Brand trust

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Brand trust")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/2008_Nike%2B_Human_Race_in_Paris_the_Start.jpg/250px-2008_Nike%2B_Human_Race_in_Paris_the_Start.jpg)](/wiki/File:2008_Nike%2B_Human_Race_in_Paris_the_Start.jpg)

**Nike:** Associated with athleticism, performance, and motivation, Nike's brand image resonates with both professional athletes and fitness enthusiasts.

Brand trust is the intrinsic 'believability' that any entity evokes. In the commercial world, the intangible aspect of brand trust impacts the behavior and performance of its business stakeholders in many intriguing ways. It creates the foundation of a strong brand connect with all stakeholders, converting simple awareness to strong commitment.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

A positive brand image fosters trust among consumers, leading to increased loyalty and repeat purchases. When consumers perceive a brand positively, they are more likely to choose it over competitors.[[110]](#cite_note-:0-110)

#### Market Differentiation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brand&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Market Differentiation")]

A distinctive brand image helps a company stand out in a competitive market, making its products or services more recognizable and appealing.[[110]](#cite_note-:0-110)
